Event: John makes it clear Jamie’s only value is as ranch legal representation
Explanation: He secures legal services and control while deeply devaluing Jamie, damaging Jamie with no real cost to himself at the time.

Event: John allows Beth’s physical abuse of Jamie
Explanation: He maintains Beth’s loyalty and family power dynamics while letting Jamie be harmed with no benefit to Jamie.

Event: Beth physically abuses Jamie
Explanation: Beth vents rage and asserts dominance at Jamie’s expense, gaining emotional release and control while only harming him.

Event: John realizes the ramifications of Beth blackmailing Jamie over the murder of Jamie’s father
Explanation: Insight creates internal conflict and no practical gain, and it doesn’t stop the abuse so any benefit to Jamie is minimal.

Event: Beth functionally blackmails Jamie over murdering his real father
Explanation: She gains leverage and control over Jamie while leaving him vulnerable and traumatized, with little upside for him.

Event: John comments he still loves Jamie regardless
Explanation: Verbal reassurance gives John some emotional relief and Jamie marginal comfort but doesn’t alter abusive dynamics.

Event: John returns to abusing Jamie as before
Explanation: He reasserts dominance and vents spite with no self-cost in the short term while continuing to harm Jamie.

Event: John robs Jamie of his efforts to be Governor out of spite
Explanation: He blocks Jamie’s political ascent to keep control yet later loses from this choice; Jamie is clearly harmed.

Event: Jamie declares John’s impeachment from being Governor
Explanation: Jamie gains power and revenge while destabilizing John and the family, causing broad collateral damage.

Event: Jamie tacitly suggests Sarah use her contacts to have John killed
Explanation: He seeks freedom and safety from John but is wracked by guilt and fallout, and John is gravely harmed.

Event: Sarah tells Jamie to stop crying after John’s murder
Explanation: She keeps Jamie focused on her agenda and power play while emotionally invalidating him.

Event: John falls down from a ruptured ulcer
Explanation: His prior stress and lifestyle lead to serious self-harm with no gain, though others react caringly.

Event: Ranch-hands rush John to the on-site veterinarian
Explanation: They expend effort and worry to save his life, benefiting him greatly with minor personal cost.

Event: John undergoes diagnosis and treatment for colon cancer
Explanation: Treatment is painful and frightening with uncertain benefit; he at least preserves a chance to survive.

Event: Militia members shoot John multiple times
Explanation: They nearly kill him but fail long-term and provoke lethal retaliation, causing harm with no durable benefit.

Event: John kills members of the Montana Free Militia after they threaten his family
Explanation: He protects his family and deterrence at the cost of others’ lives and escalation risks.

Event: John disagrees with outsiders on principle and disregards useful ranching practices
Explanation: His contrarianism undermines his own and the ranch’s interests while also rejecting beneficial proposals for others.

Event: John refuses to install solar panels to “protect” sage grass
Explanation: He blocks revenue and modernization for a useless plant, hurting both himself and wider economic/environmental gains.

Event: John ruthlessly uses violence (“train station”) to secure his rule
Explanation: He preserves power and territory through killings that create fear, bodies, and a bad reputation for others.

Event: John runs his family with an iron fist and no compromise
Explanation: His rigidity eventually tears the family apart and ruins their reputation, harming both him and them.

Event: John tells Jamie suicide is the most shameful thing a man can do
Explanation: He discourages suicide with shaming, giving Jamie some reason to live while inflicting lasting emotional damage and hypocrisy.

Event: John dotes on his grandson Tate
Explanation: He gains emotional fulfillment and gives Tate love and attention, benefiting them both.

Event: John allows Beth to abuse Jamie and only mildly asks her to “take it easy” once
Explanation: He preserves Beth’s loyalty and his quiet status quo at Jamie’s continued expense.

Event: John gives Beth no ire when she admits pushing Jamie toward suicide
Explanation: He avoids confronting Beth to maintain their alliance while signaling Jamie’s suffering doesn’t matter.

Event: John irrationally hates and regularly abuses Jamie
Explanation: He indulges his resentment and vents frustration while deeply damaging Jamie and eventually himself.

Event: Hitmen murder John and stage it as suicide
Explanation: They complete the job but trigger cascading consequences they do not seem to profit from within this account.

Event: John puts aside differences with Rainwater and Jenkins to go after the Beck clan
Explanation: He protects his family and area while curbing a worse threat, benefiting both himself and the valley.

Event: John hosts a sit-down meeting with Rainwater and Jenkins to coordinate against the Becks
Explanation: He forges a pragmatic alliance that improves everyone’s ability to fight a mutual enemy.

Event: John and Rainwater ally again to oppose Market Equities
Explanation: They both strengthen their positions and resist an external corporate threat.

Event: John mercy-kills a mortally-wounded horse after an auto accident
Explanation: He suffers emotional loss to end the animal’s suffering, clearly benefiting the horse.

Event: John investigates the crash scene, asserting authority
Explanation: He maintains control and ensures matters are handled his way, and the scene benefits from experienced oversight.

Event: John keeps the family cowboy code and “things we do to keep you fed” attitude
Explanation: He gains power and identity, while his harsh methods extract obedience and fear from family and workers.

Event: John cannot comprehend why Jamie would turn on him despite years of abuse
Explanation: His blindness undermines his own safety and precludes repairing the relationship, harming both sides.

Event: Jamie impeaches John as governor
Explanation: He frees himself and gains leverage while destabilizing the family and state politics.

Event: John allows Beth to continue abusing Jamie for defending himself
Explanation: He upholds Beth’s primacy while confirming Jamie’s helpless status and trauma.

Event: John orders Rip to murder a mortician to destroy evidence
Explanation: He protects himself and the ranch from legal risk at the cost of an innocent life.

Event: Jamie reveals John’s dirty secrets leading to impeachment
Explanation: He gains power and vengeance, while damaging John’s image and the family’s stability.

Event: Market Equities/Jamie exploit tax/legal problems to strip the ranch’s assets
Explanation: They gain legal and financial leverage at the direct loss of the Duttons’ assets and power.

Event: Kayce and Beth sell equipment and animals to pay debts
Explanation: They reduce their own long-term capacity and legacy to stave off more immediate financial collapse.

Event: Kayce decides to sell the Yellowstone ranch to Rainwater
Explanation: He sacrifices his inheritance and father’s wish to secure some financial/strategic stability and tribal benefit.

Event: John refuses Market Equities’ massive cash offer for the land
Explanation: He preserves pride and promise while dooming the family financially, helping no one in the end.

Event: John insists on holding on to the land as ancestors once did
Explanation: His obstinacy eventually destroys the ranch’s viability and his family’s future.

Event: John uses Jamie solely as “a lawyer on the inside” when talking him out of suicide
Explanation: He keeps a useful asset alive for legal cover with little regard for Jamie’s wellbeing.

Event: John has Kayce hand-picked as new Livestock Commissioner
Explanation: He secures institutional power and boosts Kayce’s status but contributes to nepotism and potential governance issues.

Event: John installs Jamie as Attorney General
Explanation: He gains legal control and gives Jamie high office, yet does so self-servingly and sets up later conflict.

Event: John stops to help a woman and her son with a flat tire
Explanation: He risks exposure to danger but offers significant help; harm only comes because others attack.

Event: Militia member shoots John and the woman he tried to help
Explanation: They cause suffering and chaos with no articulated benefit, and fail to eliminate John permanently.

Event: John becomes governor primarily to protect the ranch
Explanation: He gains power and shields the ranch while neglecting broader public responsibilities.

Event: John abdicates gubernatorial duties to capture and brand cattle
Explanation: He prioritizes ranch chores over governing, hurting his legitimacy and underserving the public.

Event: John ignores his advisors as governor
Explanation: His refusal to listen weakens his own administration and the quality of governance.

Event: John manipulates his family to serve his goals
Explanation: He consolidates control and advances his agenda while eroding their autonomy and trust.

Event: John refuses to support Kayce’s move to empower Rainwater’s investigation
Explanation: His resistance strains relations and slows a just investigation, but Kayce’s decision still benefits victims.

Event: Kayce defers livestock officers to help Rainwater investigate a missing native woman
Explanation: He risks John’s ire but advances justice and tribal authority.

Event: Jamie finally sees John as a millstone dragging him down
Explanation: The insight hurts him emotionally yet sets up his eventual push for change, with limited broader impact.

Event: John forgets or ignores the damage his family has done while moralizing
Explanation: His double standards undercut his credibility and worsen conflicts without benefit.

Event: John starts a relationship with Governor Lynette Perry
Explanation: He gains personal and political benefits while she gains companionship and alliance.

Event: John starts a relationship with Summer after helping her skirt charges
Explanation: He wins a lover and influence while compromising legal fairness and reinforcing his own power.

Event: John allies with Rainwater and others against Market Equities after his death (indirect legacy effect)
Explanation: His death triggers circumstances that ruin Market Equities’ plans and help preserve the land, albeit unintentionally.

Event: John keeps Jamie around despite hating him because he’s useful legally
Explanation: He exploits Jamie’s skills while continuing emotional abuse, giving Jamie only a role, not care.

Event: John serves as a father figure to Rip
Explanation: He gains a fiercely loyal surrogate son and Rip gains stability and belonging.

Event: John gives his blessing for Rip to marry Beth
Explanation: He secures family continuity and emotional bonds, which benefit both the couple and himself.

Event: John chooses “ranch before anything else” and rarely shows empathy beyond it
Explanation: His priority structure erodes relationships and legacy, harming both him and his loved ones.

Event: Jamie indirectly arranges circumstances leading to John’s staged-suicide death
Explanation: He removes his abuser and potential threat but incurs guilt and massive collateral damage.

Event: Kayce plans to sell the ranch to Rainwater in defiance of John’s wishes
Explanation: He sacrifices family heritage to gain financial/ethical stability and support tribal stewardship.

Event: Beth and Kayce ultimately sell the ranch to Rainwater
Explanation: They lose ownership and identity but ensure the land is protected and creditors paid.
